walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Village Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Above and Below ready to play! First, unfold the large village board and place it in the center of the table. This board has the reputation track running along the top, the advancement track along the side, and spaces in the middle where building cards will be available for purchase. Everyone will be looking at this board throughout the game, so make sure it is easy for all players to reach.

walkthrough.title: Separate the Building Cards
walkthrough.text: Now let's sort the building cards. There are two types: houses and outposts. Houses have a tan border and are built above ground on your player board. Outposts have a dark cave border and are built underground in your cave slots. Shuffle each deck separately and place them face down near the village board.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Building Row
walkthrough.text: Flip house cards face up and place them on the building row spaces on the village board. The number of cards depends on your player count. For a four player game, fill all the spaces. These are the buildings available for anyone to purchase on their turn. Whenever someone buys a building, you will refill the empty space at the end of the round.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Cave Cards
walkthrough.text: Take the outpost cards and create a face-down deck near the board. When a player successfully explores, they will draw an outpost card as part of their reward. Each outpost card shows a cave space where you can build it on your player board. Keep this deck within reach of everyone because exploring is a big part of the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Goods Supply
walkthrough.text: Find all the goods tokens. There are four types: fish, mushrooms, amethyst, and ore. Place them in separate piles near the village board. These are the resources you will collect by harvesting from your buildings. Each type of good is worth different amounts of coins when sold and different amounts of points at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Coin Supply
walkthrough.text: Put all the coin tokens in a central supply. Each player will start with a specific number of coins depending on player count. In a four player game, each player starts with four coins. Coins are used to buy buildings, train new villagers, and purchase goods from other players. Keep the supply easily accessible because coins change hands constantly.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Villager Row
walkthrough.text: Now let's prepare the available villagers. Find the villager cards. Each card shows a villager with one or more skill icons: a hammer for building, a quill for training, or a lantern for exploring. Shuffle the villager deck and deal face-up villager cards onto the villager row on the village board. These are the villagers available for training this round.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player a personal player board. Your board has two sections. The top section is your village above ground with spaces for houses. The bottom section has slots for cave outpost cards. You will also see bed icons on your board. Beds are critical for recovering injured and exhausted villagers between rounds. Count your starting beds carefully because you start with three.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Villagers
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with three villager cards. Find the starting villager sets marked with a player icon on the back. Each player takes their set and places all three villagers face up in the ready area beside their player board. Ready villagers stand upright. These are your workers for the first round, so take a moment to check what skill icons each one has.

walkthrough.title: Place Reputation and Advancement Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a scoring token in their color and places it on the starting space of the reputation track on the village board. Also place a token on the starting space of the advancement track. Reputation earns bonus victory points at the end of the game. The advancement track unlocks rewards as you progress, like extra beds and bonus coins.

walkthrough.title: Grab the Encounter Book
walkthrough.text: Find the encounter book. This is the spiral-bound storybook with numbered paragraphs. When someone explores a cave, another player will read an encounter from this book. Each encounter gives you a choice between different options with different difficulty thresholds. Do not read ahead in the book. The surprises are half the fun.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Explore Die
walkthrough.text: This black die has lantern icons on its faces. When you send villagers to explore, each explorer rolls this die to see how many lanterns they contribute. You need enough total lanterns to meet the encounter threshold for your chosen option. Place the die in the center of the table where everyone can grab it.

walkthrough.title: Set the Round Tracker
walkthrough.text: Place the round marker on space one of the round track on the village board. The game lasts exactly seven rounds. At the end of each round, you will advance this marker one space. When it reaches seven, you finish that round and then count up final scores. Seven rounds goes faster than you think, so make every action count.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a random starting player and give them the first player token. Play proceeds clockwise from the first player. The first player token passes clockwise at the end of each round. Now you are all set up and ready to learn the rules. Let's build our villages above and below!

walkthrough.title: Game Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about how the game flows. Above and Below is played over exactly seven rounds. Each round, players take turns clockwise, performing one action per turn. When you do not want to take any more actions, you pass. Once all players have passed, the round ends, everyone recovers their villagers, and you move to the next round.

walkthrough.title: The Five Main Actions
walkthrough.text: On your turn you choose one of five actions: explore, build, harvest, train, or labor. Each action requires you to exhaust one or more of your ready villagers. Once a villager is exhausted, they cannot be used again until the recovery phase at the end of the round. Let's go through each action one at a time.

walkthrough.title: Explore Action
walkthrough.text: Exploring is the heart of the game. To explore, you must send at least two ready villagers into the caves. Exhaust all villagers you send. Each exploring villager rolls the explore die once to see how many lanterns they contribute. Then another player picks up the encounter book, draws a random encounter card for the paragraph number, and reads it aloud.

walkthrough.title: Encounter Choices
walkthrough.text: Each encounter gives you multiple options, usually two or three. Each option shows a lantern threshold you need to meet and a reward if you succeed. Higher thresholds give better rewards but are harder to reach. Add up all the lanterns your villagers rolled. If your total meets or exceeds the threshold, you succeed and claim the reward. If you fail, you get nothing from that option.

walkthrough.title: Explore Rewards and Injuries
walkthrough.text: When you succeed at an encounter, you typically earn coins, goods, reputation, or advancement. You also draw a cave card, which is an outpost you can build later. Here is the important part: some encounter options can injure your villagers. Injured villagers need a bed and take longer to recover. You must weigh the risk of injury against the potential reward.

walkthrough.title: Build Action
walkthrough.text: To build, you need a ready villager with a hammer icon. Exhaust that villager, pay the coin cost shown on the building card, and place the building on your player board. Houses go in your village section above ground. Outposts go in your cave section below ground, but you must have an open cave slot from a previous explore to place an outpost.

walkthrough.title: Building Benefits
walkthrough.text: Buildings are the backbone of your engine. Many houses produce goods when you harvest. Some buildings give you extra beds for faster recovery. Others give end-game victory points, advancement, or reputation. When you place a building, check its icons carefully. A building with a bed icon immediately gives you an extra bed for recovery each round.

walkthrough.title: Harvest Action
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about harvesting. To harvest, exhaust one or more ready villagers. For each villager you exhaust, you may collect one good from one of your buildings that produces goods. Match the good type shown on the building. Take the matching token from the supply and place it near your player board. Goods are worth both coins if sold and victory points at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Train Action
walkthrough.text: Training lets you recruit new villagers. To train, you need a ready villager with a quill icon. Exhaust that villager and pay the coin cost shown on the villager card you want to recruit from the villager row. Take that villager and place them in your exhausted area. They will be ready next round after recovery. Refill the villager row at the end of the round.

walkthrough.title: Labor Action
walkthrough.text: Labor is the simplest action. Exhaust one ready villager to gain one coin from the supply. It is not glamorous, but sometimes you need just one more coin to afford a crucial building or recruit. Think of labor as your fallback when no other action makes sense this turn.

walkthrough.title: Free Actions
walkthrough.text: In addition to your main action, you can perform free actions on your turn. You can buy a good from another player for a minimum of three coins. You can list one of your goods for sale so other players know it is available. You can also pay one coin to refresh the building row by discarding the current cards and dealing new ones. Free actions do not exhaust villagers.

walkthrough.title: Passing Your Turn
walkthrough.text: When you do not want to take any more actions, you pass. Once you pass, you are done for the round. You cannot take any more actions even if other players are still going. If all your villagers are exhausted or injured, passing is your only option. Choose wisely when to pass because you might miss opportunities if you pass too early.

walkthrough.title: Recovery Phase
walkthrough.text: At the end of each round comes recovery. Each bed on your player board lets you recover one villager. Exhausted villagers become ready after using one bed. Injured villagers need one bed to move from injured to exhausted, and another bed the next round to move from exhausted to ready. So injured villagers take two full rounds to fully recover.

walkthrough.title: Beds and Recovery Planning
walkthrough.text: You start with three beds, so you can only recover three villagers each round. If you have more exhausted or injured villagers than beds, some will stay exhausted until the next round. This is why building structures with bed icons is so important. More beds mean more villagers ready to work each round, which means more actions.

walkthrough.title: Potions and Cider
walkthrough.text: Some encounter rewards and building bonuses give you potions or cider tokens. A potion instantly recovers one exhausted villager back to ready, bypassing the bed requirement. Cider works similarly but can also heal an injured villager directly to exhausted. These are valuable because they let you stretch your workforce beyond your bed count.

walkthrough.title: The Advancement Track
walkthrough.text: As you gain advancement from encounters and buildings, you move up the advancement track. This track gives you milestone rewards like extra coins, additional beds, or bonus goods. Moving up the track also earns you victory points at the end of the game. Keep an eye on the next milestone so you can plan actions that push you forward.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Scoring
walkthrough.text: After seven rounds, it is time to score. Count victory points from your buildings, your position on the advancement track, and the value of your goods. Then check the reputation track. The player with the most reputation earns five bonus victory points, second place gets three, and third place gets two. Add it all up, including any building bonus effects.

walkthrough.title: Tiebreakers and Winning
walkthrough.text: The player with the most total victory points wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most coins wins. If still tied, the player with the most villagers wins. And if it is still tied after that, the player with the most buildings wins. Now let's see how a real round plays out in the practice tutorial!

walkthrough.title: Meet Our Four Players
walkthrough.text: We have Alice, Ben, Carla, and Dan. Each player has three starting villagers and four coins. Alice has two villagers with lantern icons and one with a hammer. Ben has one with a quill, one with a hammer, and one with a lantern. Carla has a similar mix, and Dan is eager to explore.

walkthrough.title: Alice Explores the Caves
walkthrough.text: Alice goes first and decides to explore. She sends her two lantern villagers into the caves. She exhausts both villagers and rolls the explore die once for each. Her first villager rolls two lanterns and her second rolls one lantern, giving her a total of three lanterns. Ben picks up the encounter book and reads the encounter aloud.

walkthrough.title: Alice Chooses Her Encounter Option
walkthrough.text: The encounter offers two options. Option one requires two lanterns and rewards one coin plus a mushroom. Option two requires four lanterns and rewards two coins, a reputation point, and a fish. Alice has three lanterns. She picks option one since she cannot meet the four lantern threshold. She collects one coin and one mushroom, then draws a cave card for a potential outpost.

walkthrough.title: Ben Trains a New Villager
walkthrough.text: Ben wants more workers. He exhausts his quill villager and pays three coins to recruit a villager from the villager row. The new recruit has a lantern icon, which will be great for future explorations. The new villager goes straight to Ben's exhausted area. Ben will not be able to use the new recruit until next round after recovery.

walkthrough.title: Carla Builds a House
walkthrough.text: Carla spots a house in the building row that produces fish and has a bed icon. She exhausts her hammer villager and pays the coin cost. She places the house on an open village space on her player board. The bed icon is important because Carla now has four beds instead of three. That extra bed means she can recover one more villager per round going forward.

walkthrough.title: Dan's Turn: Eager to Explore
walkthrough.text: Dan has been watching Alice explore and wants to try it himself. He looks at his villagers. He has two with lantern icons and one with a hammer. He is tempted to send all three villagers to explore for maximum lanterns. But wait, that would exhaust every single villager he has on turn one of the round.

walkthrough.title: Dan Sends All Three Villagers to Explore
walkthrough.text: Dan goes all in and exhausts all three villagers to explore. He rolls three dice and gets a total of five lanterns, which is a great result. The encounter offers a high-threshold option worth a reputation point, two coins, and an amethyst. Dan takes it and succeeds easily. He is thrilled with the big reward and draws a cave card.

walkthrough.title: Dan's Encounter Injures a Villager
walkthrough.text: But the encounter also specifies that one of Dan's villagers becomes injured. The encounter text marked the risk clearly, but Dan was focused on the big reward and did not consider the consequences. Now one of his three villagers is injured instead of just exhausted. This is about to cause Dan a big problem that he has not thought through yet.

walkthrough.title: Mistake One: Over-Exerting in Early Explore
walkthrough.text: Here is what Dan did wrong. He sent all three villagers to explore on turn one, leaving zero ready villagers for the rest of the round. He cannot build, harvest, train, or labor. His only option is to pass immediately on every remaining turn this round. He traded an entire round of flexibility for one big explore action. In the early game, keeping at least one villager ready gives you options.

walkthrough.title: Lesson One: Pace Your Villagers
walkthrough.text: Let's learn from Dan's mistake. In the early rounds, you do not have many villagers or beds. Sending everyone to explore on turn one leaves you locked out for the rest of the round. A better approach is to send two villagers to explore and keep one ready for a build, harvest, or train action. Balance your exploration ambition against the rest of your round.

walkthrough.title: Round Continues: Alice Builds
walkthrough.text: Alice still has her hammer villager ready. She exhausts the hammer villager and buys a house from the building row that is worth three victory points. Smart move. While Dan is stuck with all villagers exhausted, Alice is still making progress on her second turn of the round. This is the advantage of pacing your villagers across multiple actions.

walkthrough.title: Ben Labors for a Coin
walkthrough.text: Ben exhausts his hammer villager to labor, gaining one coin. He spent most of his coins training earlier, so he needs to rebuild his treasury. Ben still has his lantern villager ready for next turn if he wants to do something else. He will not be able to explore alone since exploring requires at least two villagers, but he could harvest or build if the right opportunity comes up.

walkthrough.title: End of Round: Recovery Phase
walkthrough.text: Alice has two exhausted villagers and three beds. She recovers both easily and has a bed to spare. Ben has three exhausted villagers and three beds. He recovers all three perfectly. Carla has one exhausted villager and four beds. She recovers easily with beds to spare.

walkthrough.title: Dan's Recovery Problem
walkthrough.text: Now let's look at Dan. He has two exhausted villagers and one injured villager. He only has three beds. He uses two beds to recover his two exhausted villagers from exhausted to ready. He uses his third bed to move his injured villager from injured to exhausted. That injured villager will not be ready until the end of next round. Dan is starting round two with only two ready villagers instead of three.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Two: Ignoring Bed Count
walkthrough.text: Dan's second mistake is related to the first. He did not consider his bed count before choosing the risky encounter option. With only three beds and three villagers, an injury means one villager will be out for two full rounds. If Dan had checked his bed count first, he might have picked the safer encounter option. He would have earned less but kept all his villagers available next round.

walkthrough.title: Lesson Two: Check Your Beds Before Risky Encounters
walkthrough.text: Before choosing a risky encounter option, count your beds and your villagers. If you only have enough beds to recover your exhausted villagers, an injury will cost you a worker for two rounds. That is a steep price in a seven round game. Build houses with bed icons early so you have recovery capacity for the risks you want to take.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Dan Wants to Build an Outpost
walkthrough.text: It is worth four victory points and produces amethyst. He wants to build it right away. He has a hammer villager ready and enough coins. He exhausts his hammer villager and announces he is building the outpost in his caves.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Three: No Open Cave Slot
walkthrough.text: The other players stop Dan. To build an outpost underground, you need an open cave slot on your player board. Cave slots come from successfully exploring and placing cave cards. Dan drew a cave card, but that cave card is the outpost itself. He needs a different cave card with an open building space already on his player board. Dan cannot build this outpost because he has no open cave slot to place it on.

walkthrough.title: Lesson Three: Explore Before You Build Below
walkthrough.text: Outposts need open cave slots on your player board. You earn cave slots by exploring successfully, and some cave cards provide empty slots for future outposts. Plan your explore actions to build up cave infrastructure before buying expensive outposts. Think of exploring as unlocking real estate underground that you can develop later.

walkthrough.title: Dan Adjusts and Builds a House Instead
walkthrough.text: Dan cannot build his outpost, but he can still build a house above ground. He picks a house from the building row that has a bed icon. Smart adjustment. Now he will have four beds for recovery, which will help him take riskier encounter options in future rounds. He has learned to build his infrastructure before going for big plays.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Great job following along! You just watched Dan make three common mistakes and learn from each one. Pace your villagers so you are not locked out for an entire round. Check your bed count before choosing risky encounters because injuries are expensive. And make sure you have open cave slots before trying to build outposts underground. These three lessons will save you a lot of frustration in your first real game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you head into your first game of Above and Below, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy advice on balancing exploration with village building.

Second, reference the Appendix for quick lookups on actions, villager states, and scoring.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Now go explore those caves!

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check out General Tips → balancing exploration with village building
Reference the Appendix → actions, villager states, scoring rules
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Build Beds Early
walkthrough.text: The single most impactful thing you can do in the first two rounds is increase your bed count. You start with three beds and three villagers, which means zero recovery margin. The moment you train a fourth villager or take an injury, you are bottlenecked. Prioritize buying houses with bed icons in rounds one and two. Going from three beds to five beds transforms your entire game because you can explore aggressively, take risks on encounters, and still recover everyone each round.

walkthrough.title: Balance Above and Below
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to focus entirely on exploring caves for the narrative fun and big rewards. But the best scores come from combining above-ground houses with underground outposts. Houses give you steady goods production and beds. Outposts give you high victory point values and unique bonuses. Aim for a mix of both rather than going all in on one strategy. A player who only explores will lack beds and production, while a player who only builds above will miss out on high-value outpost points.

walkthrough.title: Train Villagers Before Round Three
walkthrough.text: You start with three villagers, but competitive players will have five or six by mid-game. Each new villager gives you one more action per round, and that advantage compounds every round. Try to train at least one new villager by the end of round two. Look for villagers with lantern icons since exploring is both rewarding and fun. A quill villager is also valuable because they let you keep training more villagers without exhausting your existing trainers.

walkthrough.title: Read Encounter Options Carefully
walkthrough.text: When the encounter book is read aloud, listen to all the options before choosing. Some options have hidden costs like injuries or lost goods that are easy to miss in the excitement of big rewards. Also pay attention to the lantern threshold relative to your roll. If you have exactly the threshold, you will succeed, but with no margin for error. Sometimes taking the safer lower-threshold option gives you a guaranteed reward instead of risking failure on a higher option.

walkthrough.title: Goods Are Worth Points, Not Just Coins
walkthrough.text: New players often sell their goods for quick coins, but goods are also worth victory points at the end of the game. Before selling a good to another player for three coins, consider whether keeping it for end-game scoring might be worth more. Amethyst and ore are particularly valuable. Only sell goods when you desperately need coins for a building or recruit that will generate more points than the good itself.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Reputation Track
walkthrough.text: Reputation is easy to forget but worth significant points. Five victory points for first place, three for second, and two for third is a major swing. If you are tied for reputation or one point behind the leader, a single encounter that grants reputation could jump you ahead. Keep an eye on where all players sit on the reputation track, especially in the last two rounds. Sometimes one explore action focused on reputation can be the difference between winning and losing.

walkthrough.title: Use the Refresh Building Row Wisely
walkthrough.text: For just one coin, you can discard the current building row and deal new cards. This is a free action, so it does not cost you a villager. If nothing in the building row fits your strategy, spend the coin and refresh. But be strategic about timing. Refreshing early in the round gives all players access to the new buildings. Sometimes it is better to wait until right before your build action so you get first pick of the new cards.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Final Two Rounds
walkthrough.text: Rounds six and seven are your last chances to score. By round six, stop investing in infrastructure like beds and training unless the payoff is immediate. Focus on building high-point value buildings, harvesting goods for end-game points, and making one or two last explore runs for reputation or advancement. Count your projected score and compare it to what you can see of other players. The final rounds are about maximizing points, not building your engine.

summary.title: Build Beds Early
bullets: Start with 3 beds = zero recovery margin
Prioritize bed-icon houses in rounds 1-2
5 beds → explore aggressively + recover everyone

summary.title: Balance Above and Below
bullets: Houses: steady goods production + beds
Outposts: high VP + unique bonuses
Mix of both outscores going all-in on one

summary.title: Train Villagers Before Round Three
bullets: More villagers = more actions per round (compounds over time)
Train at least 1 by end of round 2
Lantern villagers best for exploring; quill for chain-training

summary.title: Read Encounter Options Carefully
bullets: Listen to ALL options before choosing
Watch for hidden costs: injuries, lost goods
Guaranteed lower reward often beats risky higher reward

summary.title: Goods Are Worth Points, Not Just Coins
bullets: Goods = VP at end of game, not just coins when sold
Amethyst and ore especially valuable
Only sell when coins fund something worth more than the good

summary.title: Watch the Reputation Track
bullets: 5/3/2 VP for 1st/2nd/3rd in reputation — major swing
Track all players' reputation, especially rounds 6-7
One reputation-focused explore can decide the game

summary.title: Use the Refresh Building Row Wisely
bullets: 1 coin free action — no villager cost
Refresh before your build action for first pick
Refreshing early gives opponents access to new cards too

summary.title: Plan Your Final Two Rounds
bullets: Rounds 6-7: stop investing in infrastructure
Focus on high-VP buildings, goods harvesting, reputation pushes
Count projected scores — maximize points over engine

walkthrough.title: The Bed Economy Engine
walkthrough.text: Advanced players understand that beds are the true currency of Above and Below. Every bed you add lets you take one more risky action per round. The strongest opening is to buy a bed-icon house in round one, train a villager in round two, and then start aggressive exploring in round three with five villagers and five beds. This engine lets you explore with three villagers while keeping two for building and harvesting, and still recover everyone at round end.

walkthrough.title: Encounter Probability and Threshold Math
walkthrough.text: Each villager rolls the explore die, which has faces showing zero, one, two, or three lanterns. The average roll is about one and a half lanterns per villager. With two villagers you should expect about three lanterns. With three villagers, expect about four to five. Knowing these averages lets you choose encounter options with confidence. If an option requires four lanterns and you sent three villagers, you have a strong chance of success. Do not gamble on five lantern thresholds with only two explorers.

walkthrough.title: Advancement Track Rush
walkthrough.text: The advancement track gives increasingly powerful rewards as you climb. Players who rush advancement early get compounding benefits like extra beds, free goods, and bonus coins that fuel everything else. Focus your first few encounters on options that grant advancement rather than raw coins. The track rewards are worth far more over the course of a seven round game than a couple of extra coins. Pair this with buildings that also grant advancement to climb the track fastest.

walkthrough.title: Goods Portfolio Optimization
walkthrough.text: Not all goods are created equal. Amethyst and ore score more points than fish and mushrooms. When choosing which buildings to buy and which encounters to pursue, prioritize actions that yield high-value goods. Build a production chain where your houses generate the goods you want, and then harvest them consistently. Selling low-value goods like fish for coins to fund high-value outpost buildings is often the optimal conversion.

walkthrough.title: Reputation Timing
walkthrough.text: Reputation gives five, three, or two victory points to first, second, and third place. Smart players track reputation carefully and time their pushes. If you are already in first place, there is diminishing value in gaining more reputation. If you are in fourth place, one or two reputation points might jump you into scoring range. The best time to push reputation is in round five or six when the standings are clearer and opponents have fewer rounds to respond.

walkthrough.title: Villager Composition Matters
walkthrough.text: Your starting villagers have a fixed mix of skills, but your recruited villagers shape your strategy. If you are going heavy on exploration, recruit lantern villagers. If you want to build rapidly, recruit hammer villagers. The most flexible villager has multiple icons. Recruiting a villager with both a lantern and a hammer lets you explore one round and build the next without being locked into a single strategy. Evaluate the villager row each round for multi-icon recruits.

walkthrough.title: Deny Key Buildings
walkthrough.text: In a competitive game, watch what your opponents need. If someone is building a cave empire and a powerful outpost appears in the building row, consider buying it yourself even if it is not your top priority. Denying a four or five point building to an opponent can be worth more than buying your ideal three point building. Similarly, refreshing the building row when it favors your opponents and hurts nobody else can shift the game in your favor.

walkthrough.title: The Seven-Round Clock
walkthrough.text: Everything in Above and Below should be evaluated through the lens of the seven-round clock. A building bought in round one produces goods six times. A building bought in round six produces goods once. A villager trained in round one gives you six rounds of extra actions. A villager trained in round six gives you one. Compound investments early and cash out late. In rounds one through three, build your engine. In rounds four through five, run your engine at full speed. In rounds six through seven, harvest points and make your final push on reputation and advancement.

summary.title: The Bed Economy Engine
bullets: Round 1: bed-icon house → Round 2: train villager → Round 3: aggressive explore
5 beds + 5 villagers = explore 3, keep 2 for other actions, recover all

summary.title: Encounter Probability and Threshold Math
bullets: Average ~1.5 lanterns per villager per die roll
2 villagers ≈ 3 lanterns, 3 villagers ≈ 4-5 lanterns
Don't gamble on 5+ thresholds with only 2 explorers

summary.title: Advancement Track Rush
bullets: Early advancement → compounding rewards (beds, goods, coins)
Choose encounter options granting advancement over raw coins
Pair with advancement-granting buildings for fastest climb

summary.title: Goods Portfolio Optimization
bullets: Amethyst & ore > fish & mushrooms in VP value
Build production chains for high-value goods
Sell low-value goods → fund high-VP outpost buildings

summary.title: Reputation Timing
bullets: 5/3/2 VP split — diminishing returns if already in 1st
Best push window: rounds 5-6 when standings are clear
Track all opponents' reputation positions

summary.title: Villager Composition Matters
bullets: Recruit to match strategy: lanterns for exploring, hammers for building
Multi-icon villagers offer maximum flexibility
Evaluate villager row each round for best recruits

summary.title: Deny Key Buildings
bullets: Buy opponent's ideal building to deny 4-5 VP swing
Refresh building row when it favors opponents
Defensive plays often worth more than marginal offense

summary.title: The Seven-Round Clock
bullets: Rounds 1-3: build engine (beds, villagers, production)
Rounds 4-5: run engine at full speed
Rounds 6-7: harvest points, push reputation, cash out

entries.definition: Send two or more ready villagers to the caves. Exhaust all sent villagers, each rolls the explore die for lanterns, then resolve an encounter from the encounter book.
Exhaust one ready villager with a hammer icon and pay the coin cost to place a house or outpost on your player board.
Exhaust one or more ready villagers. For each villager exhausted, collect one good from one of your buildings that produces goods.
Exhaust one ready villager with a quill icon and pay the coin cost to recruit a villager from the villager row. The new villager starts exhausted.
Exhaust one ready villager to gain one coin from the supply.
Free action. Purchase a good from another player for a minimum of three coins. Does not exhaust a villager.
Free action. Announce that one of your goods is available for purchase by other players.
Free action. Pay one coin to discard all face-up buildings in the row and deal new ones from the deck.
End your participation in the current round. You cannot take any more actions until the next round begins.
A villager who is available to be exhausted for an action. Ready villagers stand upright near your player board.
A villager who has been used for an action this round. Cannot be used again until recovered. Requires one bed during the recovery phase to return to ready.
A villager hurt during an encounter. Takes two rounds to fully recover: one bed moves them from injured to exhausted, then another bed next round moves them from exhausted to ready.
A building with a tan border placed in the above-ground village section of your player board. May produce goods, provide beds, or grant victory points.
A building with a dark cave border placed in the underground cave section of your player board. Requires an open cave slot. Generally higher VP value than houses.
A recovery slot on your player board or provided by buildings. Each bed recovers one villager per round during the recovery phase. Players start with three beds.
An open space on your player board's cave section where an outpost can be built. Earned through successful explore actions and certain cave cards.
A basic good produced by certain buildings. Can be sold for coins or kept for end-game victory points.
A basic good produced by certain buildings. Can be sold for coins or kept for end-game victory points.
A high-value good produced by certain buildings and encounter rewards. Worth more victory points than fish or mushrooms at end of game.
A high-value good produced by certain buildings and encounter rewards. Worth more victory points than fish or mushrooms at end of game.
A consumable token that instantly recovers one exhausted villager to ready, bypassing the bed requirement.
A consumable token that heals one injured villager to exhausted, bypassing the normal recovery wait.
The game's currency. Used to buy buildings, train villagers, refresh the building row, and purchase goods from other players.
A narrative event read from the encounter book when a player explores. Presents multiple options with different lantern thresholds and rewards.
The spiral-bound storybook containing numbered encounter paragraphs. Read aloud by another player during explore actions.
The number of lanterns required to succeed at a chosen encounter option. Total rolled lanterns must meet or exceed this number.
The custom black die rolled by each exploring villager. Faces show zero, one, two, or three lantern icons.
Victory points printed on building cards. Scored at end of game for all houses and outposts on your player board.
A track on the village board. Moving up grants milestone rewards and victory points at end of game. Advanced through encounters and building bonuses.
A track on the village board. At end of game, most reputation earns five VP, second place earns three VP, third place earns two VP.
Victory points earned from goods you still hold at end of game. Higher-value goods like amethyst and ore score more than fish and mushrooms.
Special scoring conditions on certain buildings that grant extra victory points at end of game based on your board state.
If VP is tied: most coins wins. If still tied: most villagers. If still tied: most buildings.
One of seven game rounds. Players take clockwise turns performing one action each until all pass. Ends with a recovery phase.
End-of-round phase where beds recover villagers. Each bed recovers one villager: exhausted to ready, or injured to exhausted.
Face-up villager cards on the village board available for training. Refilled at end of each round.
Face-up building cards on the village board available for purchase. Can be refreshed for one coin as a free action.
Your personal board with an above-ground village section for houses and an underground cave section for outposts. Shows your bed count.
Villager skill icon required to perform the Build action.
Villager skill icon required to perform the Train action.
Villager skill icon associated with exploring. Lantern villagers contribute to encounter die rolls.
